text.title: Monolith Architecture Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the monolith-architecture methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: Default to a monolith when team < 10 OR domain boundaries unvalidated OR DevOps maturity below distributed-systems competence. Microservices MUST NOT be chosen as default architecture.

rationale.source: Fowler — Monolith First (2015) + 2024 microservices premature-adoption survey.

rule.rationale: Premature microservices produce distributed monoliths — the worst combination; Fowler's Monolith First holds across postmortem data.

rule.statement: When feature merges produce frequent conflicts on shared service / repository classes, adopt vertical-slice organisation: each feature owns all layers. Layered (horizontal) layout MUST be reserved for thin CRUD with a small team.

rationale.source: Jimmy Bogard — Vertical Slice Architecture; internal monolith refactor audits.

rule.rationale: Horizontal layers create high coupling across features; vertical slices contain change to one directory per feature.

rule.statement: Within a monolith, business operations MUST run inside a single database transaction unless explicit eventual-consistency is required. The transaction is the boundary, not a Saga.

rationale.source: Monolith advantage rationale; Saga complexity case studies.

rule.rationale: The dominant monolith advantage is ACID; abandoning it for Sagas reintroduces microservices complexity with none of the deploy independence.

rule.statement: Scale-up (bigger instance, faster disk, more RAM) MUST be exhausted before scale-out (multiple instances behind a load balancer). Scale-out for a monolith requires session-stateless design + shared cache + shared DB.

rationale.source: Stack Overflow architecture postmortems; cost-per-RPS studies.

rule.rationale: Scale-up reaches Stack Overflow-scale throughput on commodity hardware; scale-out introduces operational complexity that 90% of monoliths never need.

rule.statement: Extract a service from the monolith only when one of: independent scaling (10× traffic differential), independent team release cadence, polyglot need, or fault isolation requirement holds. Extraction MUST follow the strangler-fig pattern from a modular monolith — never from a big-ball-of-mud.

rationale.source: Strangler-fig pattern (Fowler); internal extraction case studies.

rule.rationale: Extracting without modular boundaries reproduces the spaghetti as a distributed system; extraction has to be earned by prior modularity.

rule.statement: When prerequisites hold (functional list, single deployable, test pyramid, single pipeline), apply r1 → r2 → r3 → r4 → r5 and emit the spec.
rule.rationale: Order encodes risk: layout first, transaction strategy second, scaling third, extraction last.

rule.statement: Do NOT use when (a) independent scaling already needed, (b) ≥10 teams with cadence conflicts, (c) polyglot mandate. Adopt modular-monolith or microservices instead.
rule.rationale: Forcing monolith ceremony in microservice-justified contexts wastes review time and produces a worse outcome.
